Article.ArticleTitle: Effects of single and double dielectric barrier discharges on electric fields, discharge characteristics, reactive species, and microbial inactivation of food pathogens.

Abstract.AbstractText: Cold atmospheric plasma (CAP) is an effective method for inactivating foodborne pathogenic microorganisms. However, the overall effectiveness of plasma discharge is inevitably influenced by the dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) dielectric layer. In this study, the differences in discharge characteristics between single dielectric layer DBD (S-DBD) and double dielectric layer DBD (D-DBD) reactors at different plasma input powers were measured based on visual images, voltage-current waveforms, Lissajous figures, temperature variations, and optical emission spectra. The simulated electric field distributions indicated that higher ionization rates and higher plasma densities were produced at high plasma input powers, explaining the effect of plasma discharges on chemically reactive species. Upon excitation with the same plasma input power, the discharge of D-DBD reactor was more uniform and stable. S-DBD reactor had stronger discharge intensity, higher charge characteristics, dielectric barrier temperature, and spectral intensity due to the effective utilization of field emission and secondary electron emission. Treatments with both dielectric structures were effective in inactivating foodborne pathogens. Specifically, treatment with S-DBD plasma at 80 W for 4 min resulted in a maximum reduction of 3.52-log10 in Salmonella typhimurium and 0.94-log10 in Listeria monocytogenes. In contrast, D-DBD plasma achieved lower reductions of 0.82-log10 and 0.78-log10 for the same pathogens. Based on the high electron density and concentration of reactive species, a rational mechanism for the conversion of reactive species in S-DBD and D-DBD reactors was proposed. Collectively, this work provides fundamental insights into the design and optimization of DBD reactors for safer and more robust microbial control.
